data.title: More than half of Instagram's ads were in Reels format in 2025—Sensor Tower
data.description:
Advertisements shown via Reels rose 35% in 2025 on Meta's Instagram (META) and made up more than half of all the ads shown on the platform, according to data from Sensor Tower.

Watching Reels made up 46% of time spent on Instagram in the U.S. last year, up from 37% in 2024. On Facebook, it rose 29%.

Sensor Tower data showed that the rise in Reels viewership contributed to a jump in overall activity on Meta's photo-sharing app. Daily active users on Instagram rose 2% from last year, helped by increased usage of Reels.

The analytics firm's data revealed that watch time for Shorts on YouTube (GOOG) (GOOGL) was flat last year but was still ahead of rivals Instagram and TikTok on mobile. Sensor Tower estimated the number of U.S. daily active users on YouTube grew 3% in 2025.

TikTok (BDNCE), however, leads in time spent per day on the app, with users averaging 81 minutes, compared with 80 minutes on YouTube and 55 minutes on Instagram.

data.title: Anthropic CEO talks potential IPO, Gemini and AI tech at Davos
data.description:
Anthropic (ANTHRO) CEO Dario Amodei talked about the disruptive AI technology, competition from Big Tech AI companies and potential IPO plans at the World Economic Forum in Davos, Switzerland.

"My view is the signature of this technology is it's gonna take us to a world where we have very high GDP growth and potentially also very high unemployment and inequality," said Amodei. "We've never had a technology that's this disruptive. So the idea that we could have five or 10% GDP growth, but also, you know, 10% unemployment, it's not logically inconsistent at all. It's just never happened that way before."

Amodei noted that in 2023 and 2024, there were concerns about AI risk and misuse, and in 2025 the political wind shifted to AI opportunity, but now again it is shifting back.

Competition

On competitive pressure, Amodei said that one of the good choices  Anthropic made early was to be a company that is focused on enterprise rather than consumer.

On the company's ability to compete against Alphabet (GOOG) (GOOGL) Google's Gemini. Amodei said that "so, you know, the enterprise strategy, Google and OpenAI (OPENAI) are fighting it out in consumer, right? It is existential to both of them, existential to OpenAI because that's their whole business, existential to Google because they have the search business and that's what's being disrupted by this, so they need to, you know, replace themselves and fight the disruption. So that's always their first priority. And, you know, they seem much more focused on that than kind of operating in the enterprise.

"It's been great to see what Gemini is capable of, you know, capable of in consumer. You know, I think they're going about it a different way," Amodei noted.

Potential IPO?

On Anthropic's IPO plan, Amodei said that they do not know for sure what they are going to do and the company is more focused on keeping the revenue curve going, making the models better and selling the models.

"I'm not saying anything novel if I say that this is an industry with very high capital demands. And, you know, that there's only so much at some point that the private markets can provide," Amodei added.

Last week it was reported that Microsoft (MSFT) has become one of Anthropic’s (ANTHRO) top customers and was recently on track to spend about $500M a year for Anthropic AI to power Microsoft products.

In November 2025, Nvidia (NVDA) and Microsoft committed to invest up to $10B and up to $5B, respectively, in the Claude chatbot maker Anthropic — which is backed by Amazon (AMZN) and Google. Anthropic also said it has committed to spend $30B on Microsoft's Azure compute capacity and to contract additional compute capacity up to one gigawatt.

Microsoft has invested over $13B in OpenAI, giving it nearly 27% stake in the ChatGPT maker.

data.title: The legal battle over prediction markets is heating up
data.description:
Massachusetts Judge Christopher Barry-Smith issued a key ruling on Tuesday that could prohibit prediction market Kalshi (KALSHI) from offering sports-event contracts within the state. The new decision set forth that the state is entitled to a preliminary injunction prohibiting Kalshi from offering sport-related event contracts in the absence of the required license under current sports wagering laws.

The state has until Wednesday to submit its proposed preliminary injunction. At a hearing on Friday, the parties will argue about the injunction.

As the legal battle against prediction markets heats up, the ruling against Kalshi (KALSHI) is pertinent to prediction market platforms offered by Robinhood Markets (HOOD) and Polymarket (POLYMARKET), as well as sports betting operators such as DraftKings (DKNG), FanDuel (FLUT), and BetMGM (MGM) (GMVHF).

Other states that have legal actions pending against prediction markets include Tennessee, New York, and Nevada.

DraftKings (DKNG) and Flutter Entertainment (FLUT) pared their losses after the Massachusetts ruling was disclosed.

data.title: Old Second Bancorp declares $0.07 dividend
data.description:
• Old Second Bancorp (OSBC) declared $0.07/share quarterly dividend, in line with previous.
  • Forward yield 1.36%
  • Payable Feb. 9; for shareholders of record Jan. 30; ex-div Jan. 30.

data.title: BHP slides after hiking cost estimate for giant Canada potash project to $8.4B
data.description:
BHP (BHP) -1.5% in Tuesday's trading after raising its total cost estimate for the Jansen phase 1 copper project in Saskatchewan to $8.4B from its previously estimated range of $7B-$7.4B, citing "construction hours and quantities of materials that were not included in previous execution cost estimates" for the latest overrun.

Jansen's rising cost offset what was a strong quarter operationally for BHP (BHP), RBC Capital analyst Kaan Peker said, adding the latest cost estimate is ~$1 billion higher than his forecast.

BHP (BHP) said the first stage is 75% complete, and it continues to guide for copper output of ~4.15M metric tons/year, with first production expected in mid-2027.

"Jansen is an important pillar in BHP's long-term growth strategy and is a long-life, low cost expandable asset that is expected to generate benefits for shareholders for decades," said Brandon Craig, the head of BHP's (BHP) Americas business.

BHP (BHP) said it continues to review its cost estimate for Jansen's second stage, and it expects to update investors in its FQ4.

The company reported FQ2 copper production of 490.5K tons, down 1% Q/Q and 4% Y/Y, but its realized price jumped 29%, as benchmark levels tracked higher and have since surpassed $13K/ton, and raised its forecast for full-year production to 1.9M-2M metric tons of copper from a prior estimate of 1.8M-2M tons.

BHP (BHP) also maintained its full-year iron ore output guidance at 284M-296M tons, after strong FQ2 production climbed 9% Q/Q and 5% Y/Y to 69.7M tons, which the company said placed it in a solid position ahead of the typically wet Q3.

data.title: Enterprise software stocks mostly down during possible risk-off trading day
data.description:
Most enterprise software stocks followed the broader market down by noon Tuesday during a possible risk-off day due in part to U.S. and Europe trade tensions surrounding Greenland.

The iShares Expanded Tech-Software Sector ETF (IGV) was down 1.2%, following the major indices, such as the tech-focused Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND), which was down 1.5%. The S&P 500 (SP500) and the Dow Jones Industrial (DJI) had both declined about 1.2% as well.  

Adobe (ADBE) continued its steady fall and had declined 1.8% by noon trading. Its share value has declined by 33% over the past year. Workday (WDAY) had slipped 1.5%, while Palantir (PLTR) inched down 0.3%. Autodesk (ADSK) had dropped 3%.

Meanwhile, Salesforce (CRM) and Zoom (ZM) had dropped slightly by 0.2% and 0.1%, respectively. However, Atlassian (TEAM) had gained 0.4%, while ServiceNow (NOW) was up 0.6%. ServiceNow announced today it entered into a multi-year collaboration with OpenAI (OPENAI) to provide enterprise customers access to OpenAI's frontier models and custom-built artificial intelligence solutions.

Monday.com (MNDY) had gained 2%.

Software cybersecurity firms were mostly in the red as well. Check Point Software Technologies (CHKP) had declined 1.2%, Fortinet (FTNT) inched down 0.1%, CrowdStrike (CRWD) had dropped 1.5% and Zscaler (ZS) had slid 1.9%.

data.title: CrowdStrike unveils plans to expand into Saudi Arabia, India, UAE
data.description:
Cybersecurity giant CrowdStrike (CRWD) unveiled plans on Tuesday with the intent of expanding further, bringing new regional clouds to Saudi Arabia, India, and the United Arab Emirates.

CrowdStrike's expansion is a part of its Global Data Sovereignty initiative, with additional countries to follow, the company said in a statement. It allows local companies and organizations to adopt and further consolidate on its CrowdStrike Falcon platform, the company added.

In addition, the companies in these regions will be “fully connected” to CrowdStrike's global telemetry and threat intelligence. They can also maintain their respective security operations and not have to deal with regional silos or blind spots, CrowdStrike added.

“Data sovereignty requirements cannot come at the cost of AI-powered security. Adversaries continue to exploit global infrastructure with novel techniques and without regard for local data sovereignty policies,” said George Kurtz, CEO and founder of CrowdStrike. “Expanding secure data sovereignty in Saudi Arabia, India, and the UAE gives organizations local data residency as part of a unified global security model, without sacrificing security or the global intelligence required to stop breaches.”

Shares of CrowdStrike, which Wedbush Securities recently described as one of its “tech winners,” were down 1.5% in midday trading.

data.title: Ray Dalio: 'The monetary order is breaking down,' recommends 5%-15% allocation in gold
data.description:
Ray Dalio, founder of Bridgewater Associates, has declared that the global monetary order is actively breaking down, citing a fundamental shift away from fiat currencies and debt as reliable stores of wealth.

“The monetary order is breaking down,” Dalio said in an interview with CNBC at Davos. “What I mean by the monetary order is that fiat currencies and debt as a store of wealth is not being held by central banks in the same way.”

Dalio pointed to gold’s remarkable performance as primary evidence of this transformation, noting that the precious metal outperformed even technology stocks last year.

“The biggest market to move last year was the gold market, far better than the tech markets,” he explained. Central banks and sovereign wealth funds worldwide are increasingly purchasing gold as a diversifier, treating it not as a speculative metal but as what Dalio called “the second largest reserve currency.”

The billionaire investor emphasized that holders of U.S. dollar-denominated debt and the United States itself are growing increasingly wary of each other, creating a dangerous dynamic. “We know that both the holders of US dollar denominated debt, which is money, and those who need it, the United States, are worried about each other,” Dalio stated.

He noted that international geopolitical conflicts are accelerating this trend, as “even allies do not want to hold each other’s debt” and “prefer to go to a hard currency.”

For his own portfolio, Dalio recommended that investors maintain between 5%-15% allocation to gold as part of a diversified strategy. “If you had no views of the markets, that’s what you would have,” he said, adding that central banks should hold an even higher percentage than they currently do. Dalio disclosed that his own tactical positioning involves tilting away from bonds and toward gold, maintaining “a greater than normal amount” of the precious metal.

Despite acknowledging gold’s significant run-up, Dalio remains bullish on the metal’s prospects. “If I look at the run up and I take the positions held by the entities that should hold what positions, they’re still short of gold,” he explained. He also expressed optimism about technological innovation, noting he wants part of his portfolio invested in “new tech creating great disruptions” while maintaining gold as a crucial diversifier.

data.title: Cantor Fitzgerald sees powerful stimulus tailwinds, but warns of 1Q market pullback
data.description:
U.S. equities continue to benefit from a powerful combination of fiscal and monetary tailwinds, according to Cantor Fitzgerald’s latest bi-weekly macro equity outlook.

Policymakers have injected substantial liquidity into the financial system, most notably through roughly $200B in mortgage-backed security purchases alongside accelerating investment tied to artificial intelligence infrastructure. Together the investment bank noted that these measures are helping underpin risk assets and reinforce the broader growth narrative. 

While the impact is most pronounced in the United States, similar dynamics are unfolding globally. China’s central bank has rolled out fresh stimulus initiatives to stabilize growth, while Japan has leaned on fiscal support to bolster domestic demand. The cumulative effect has been a meaningful infusion of capital into global markets, lifting purchasing power and supporting economic expansion. Cantor Fitzgerald expects these forces to translate into solid corporate fundamentals, with earnings growth tracking in the mid-teens. 

Despite this constructive backdrop, the firm cautions that near-term volatility remains likely. Historical market behavior suggests investors should prepare for a notable pullback in the first quarter. Cantor Fitzgerald believes such a decline could present an attractive entry point, with the S&P 500 (SP) potentially trading meaningfully lower on a year-to-date basis during that window. 

A key driver behind this view is the market’s historical performance during U.S. midterm election years. Looking back to 1942, Cantor Fitzgerald spotlighted data that showed that first-quarter drawdowns of 7% or more have occurred in 12 of the past 21 midterm cycles.

Market Tracking ETFs: (DIA), (DDM), (DOG), (DXD), (SDOW), (SPY), (VOO), (IVV), (RSP), (SSO), (UPRO), (SH), (SDS), (SPXU), (QQQ), (QQQM), (TQQQ), (QID), and (SQQQ).

data.title: Wall Street slides and yields jump as markets focus on U.S. Greenland tensions
data.description:
Wall Street’s major market averages dropped and Treasury yields jumped on Tuesday as the standoff between the U.S. and Greenland continued to heat up.

The tech-focused Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) fell the hardest as it was last down 1.5%. At the same time, the benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) was lower by 1.2% on the day, and the blue-chip Dow (DJI) was down 1.1%. 

From a sector vantage point, 10 of the S&P 500’s 11 sectors were in negative trading territory, with consumer discretionary sliding the most. Going in the other direction, energy has been the best performer as oil prices are up 1.7%.

Market volatility also surged back into focus as the Cboe Volatility Index (VIX) climbed above the 20 threshold, marking its highest level in nearly two months. 

Treasury yields jumped higher across the curve as investors sold bonds. The shorter-end U.S. 2-year Treasury yield (US2Y) moved up 1 basis point to 3.59%, while the U.S. 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) ticked up 5 basis points to 4.27%. 

“Tariff fears are back in focus and are now intertwined in geopolitical matters, and not just economic ones. While this adds a new wrinkle to the tariff issue, we believe cooler heads will prevail and that these tariff threats are being used as a negotiating tactic for control of Greenland,” Paul Stanley, chief investment officer of Granite Bay Wealth Management stated.
